From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Vehicle not powered by petrol or diesel

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Tesla_Model_3_DCA_08_2018_0286.jpg/250px-Tesla_Model_3_DCA_08_2018_0286.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tesla_Model_3_DCA_08_2018_0286.jpg)

The [Tesla Model 3](/wiki/Tesla_Model_3 "Tesla Model 3") [all-electric car](/wiki/All-electric_car "All-electric car") is the world's all-time best-selling [plug-in electric car](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_car "Plug-in electric car") with about 950,000 units sold as of March 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Piracicaba_10_2008_151_Gast_station_selling_four_fuels.jpg/250px-Piracicaba_10_2008_151_Gast_station_selling_four_fuels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piracicaba_10_2008_151_Gast_station_selling_four_fuels.jpg)

A [Brazilian](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") filling station with four alternative fuels for sale: [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") (B3), [gasohol](/wiki/Gasohol "Gasohol") (E25), [neat ethanol](/wiki/Neat_alcohol_fuel "Neat alcohol fuel") (E100), and [compressed natural gas](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas") (CNG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Volt_charging_station.jpg/250px-Volt_charging_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volt_charging_station.jpg)

As of December 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit), the [Chevrolet Volt](/wiki/Chevrolet_Volt "Chevrolet Volt") family was the world's top selling [plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid"), with global sales of about 134,500 units.[[1]](#cite_note-Top10PEVs2016-1)

An **alternative fuel vehicle** is a [motor vehicle](/wiki/Motor_vehicle "Motor vehicle") that runs on [alternative fuel](/wiki/Alternative_fuel "Alternative fuel") rather than traditional [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum")-based [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") such as [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline"), [petrodiesel](/wiki/Petrodiesel "Petrodiesel") or [liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas") ([autogas](/wiki/Autogas "Autogas")). The term typically refers to [internal combustion engine vehicles](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine_vehicle "Internal combustion engine vehicle") or [fuel cell vehicles](/wiki/Fuel_cell_vehicle "Fuel cell vehicle") that utilize [synthetic](/wiki/Synthetic_fuel "Synthetic fuel") [renewable fuels](/wiki/Renewable_fuel "Renewable fuel") such as [biofuels](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") ([ethanol fuel](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel"), [biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") and [biogasoline](/wiki/Biogasoline "Biogasoline")), [hydrogen fuel](/wiki/Hydrogen_fuel "Hydrogen fuel") or so-called "[Electrofuel](/wiki/Electrofuel "Electrofuel")". The term can also be used to describe an [electric vehicle](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") (particularly a [battery electric vehicle](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") or a [solar vehicle](/wiki/Solar_vehicle "Solar vehicle")), which should be more appropriately called an "**alternative energy vehicle**" or "**new energy vehicle**" as its [propulsion](/wiki/Propulsion "Propulsion") actually rely on [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity") rather than [motor fuel](/wiki/Motor_fuel "Motor fuel").

Vehicle engines powered by gasoline/petrol [first emerged in the 1860s and 1870s](/wiki/History_of_the_automobile "History of the automobile"); they took until the 1930s to completely dominate the original "alternative" engines driven by steam (18th century), by gases (early 19th century), or by electricity (c. 1830s). Because of a combination of factors, such as environmental and health concerns including [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and [air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution"), high oil-prices and the potential for [peak oil](/wiki/Peak_oil "Peak oil"), development of cleaner alternative fuels and advanced power systems for vehicles has become a high priority for many governments and vehicle manufacturers around the world in recent years.

[Hybrid electric vehicles](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle") such as the [Toyota Prius](/wiki/Toyota_Prius "Toyota Prius") are not actually alternative fuel vehicles, as they still use traditional fuels such as gasoline, but through advancement in [electric battery](/wiki/Electric_battery "Electric battery")/[supercapacitor](/wiki/Supercapacitor "Supercapacitor") and [motor–generator](/wiki/Motor%E2%80%93generator "Motor–generator") technologies, they have an overall better [fuel efficiency](/wiki/Fuel_efficiency "Fuel efficiency") than conventional combustion vehicles.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Other [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") efforts in alternative forms of power focus on developing [plug-in electric](/wiki/Plug-in_electric "Plug-in electric"), [range extender](/wiki/Range_extender "Range extender") and [fuel cell vehicles](/wiki/Fuel_cell_vehicle "Fuel cell vehicle"), and even [compressed-air vehicles](/wiki/Compressed-air_vehicle "Compressed-air vehicle").

An environmental analysis of the impacts of various vehicle-fuels extends beyond just operating efficiency and emissions, especially if a technology comes into wide use. A [life-cycle assessment](/wiki/Life-cycle_assessment "Life-cycle assessment") of a vehicle involves production and post-use considerations. In general, the lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions of [battery-electric vehicles](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") are lower than emissions from hydrogen, PHEV, hybrid, compressed natural gas, gasoline, and diesel vehicles.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Current deployments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Current deployments")]

As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit), there were more than 1.49 billion [motor vehicles](/wiki/Motor_vehicle "Motor vehicle") on the world's roads,[[4]](#cite_note-USeDataBook2020-4) compared with approximately 159 million alternative fuel and advanced technology vehicles that had been sold or converted worldwide at the end of 2022 and consisting of:

* Over 65 million [flex fuel](/wiki/Flex_fuel "Flex fuel") automobiles, motorcycles and [light duty trucks](/wiki/Light_duty_truck "Light duty truck") by the end of 2021, led by Brazil with 38.3 million[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) and the United States with 27 million.[[6]](#cite_note-:4-6)
* Over 26 million [plug-in electric vehicles](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicle "Plug-in electric vehicle"), 70% of which were [battery electric vehicles](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") (BEVs) and 30% of which were [plug-in hybrids](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") (PHEVs).[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) China had 13.8 million units, Europe 7.8 million, and the United States 3 million.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) In 2022, annual sales exceeded 10 million vehicles, up 55% relative to 2021.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* 24.9 million [LPG powered vehicles](/wiki/Autogas "Autogas") by December 2013, led by [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") with 3.93 million, [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") (2.4 million), and Poland (2.75 million).[[9]](#cite_note-LPG-9)
* 24.5 million [natural gas vehicles](/wiki/Natural_gas_vehicle "Natural gas vehicle") by the end of 2017, led by China (5.35 million) followed by [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") (4.0 million), [India](/wiki/India "India") (3.05 million), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") (3 million), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") (2.3 million), and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") (1.78 million).[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10) In 2015, 2.4 million units were sold.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* Over 13 million [hybrid electric vehicles](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle") as of 2019.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* 5.7 million [neat-ethanol only light-vehicles](/wiki/Neat_ethanol_vehicle "Neat ethanol vehicle") built in Brazil since 1979,[[13]](#cite_note-ANFAVEA2010-13) with 2.4 to 3.0 million vehicles still in use by 2003.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-CEPAL-15) and 1.22 million units as of December 2011.[[16]](#cite_note-Frota092012-16)
* 70,200 [fuel cell electric vehicles](/wiki/Fuel_cell_vehicle "Fuel cell vehicle") (FCEVs) powered with [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen_vehicle "Hydrogen vehicle") by the end of 2022.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) South Korea had 29,500 units, the United States 15,000, China 11,200, and Japan 7,700.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) In 2022, annual sales amounted to 15,391 vehicles.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Hydrogen FCEV sales as a percentage of market share among electric vehicles (BEVs, PHEVs and FCEVs) declined for the 6th consecutive year.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Mainstream commercial technologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Mainstream commercial technologies")]

### Flexible fuel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Flexible fuel")]

Main article: [Flexible-fuel vehicle](/wiki/Flexible-fuel_vehicle "Flexible-fuel vehicle")

See also: [Neat ethanol vehicle](/wiki/Neat_ethanol_vehicle "Neat ethanol vehicle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Six_full_flex-fuel_Brazilian_automobiles_09_2008.jpg/250px-Six_full_flex-fuel_Brazilian_automobiles_09_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Six_full_flex-fuel_Brazilian_automobiles_09_2008.jpg)

Six typical [Brazilian](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") full flex-fuel models from several carmakers, popularly known as "flex" cars, that run on any blend of [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol_(fuel) "Ethanol (fuel)") and gasoline(actually between [E20-E25](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures#E20 "Common ethanol fuel mixtures") to [E100](/wiki/Neat_alcohol_fuel "Neat alcohol fuel")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Chevrolet_Impala_FlexFuel_34_MIA_12_2008_with_logo.jpg/250px-Chevrolet_Impala_FlexFuel_34_MIA_12_2008_with_logo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chevrolet_Impala_FlexFuel_34_MIA_12_2008_with_logo.jpg)

US [E85](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures#E85 "Common ethanol fuel mixtures")FlexFuel [Chevrolet Impala](/wiki/Chevrolet_Impala "Chevrolet Impala") LT 2009

A [flexible-fuel vehicle](/wiki/Flexible-fuel_vehicle "Flexible-fuel vehicle") (FFV) or dual-fuel vehicle (DFF) is an alternative fuel automobile or [light duty truck](/wiki/Light_duty_truck "Light duty truck") with a [multifuel](/wiki/Multifuel "Multifuel") engine that can use more than one [fuel](/wiki/Fuel "Fuel"), usually mixed in the same tank, and the blend is burned in the [combustion chamber](/wiki/Combustion_chamber "Combustion chamber") together. These vehicles are [colloquially](/wiki/Colloquial "Colloquial") called **flex-fuel**, or **flexifuel** in Europe, or just flex in Brazil. FFVs are distinguished from [bi-fuel vehicles](/wiki/Bi-fuel_vehicle "Bi-fuel vehicle"), where two fuels are stored in separate tanks. The most common commercially available FFV in the world market is the [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel") [flexible-fuel vehicle](/wiki/Flexible-fuel_vehicle "Flexible-fuel vehicle"), with the major markets concentrated in the United States, Brazil, Sweden, and some other European countries.

Ethanol flexible-fuel vehicles have standard gasoline engines that are capable of running with [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel") and gasoline mixed in the same tank. These mixtures have "E" numbers which describe the percentage of ethanol in the mixture, for example, E85 is 85% ethanol and 15% gasoline. (See [common ethanol fuel mixtures](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures "Common ethanol fuel mixtures") for more information.) Though technology exists to allow ethanol FFVs to run on any mixture up to E100,[[18]](#cite_note-SusEthanol-18)[[19]](#cite_note-CleanCity-19) in the U.S. and Europe, flex-fuel vehicles are optimized to run on [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85"). This limit is set to avoid cold starting problems during very cold weather.

Over 65 million [flex fuel](/wiki/Flex_fuel "Flex fuel") automobiles, motorcycles and [light duty trucks](/wiki/Light_duty_truck "Light duty truck") by the end of 2021, led by Brazil with 38.3 million[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) and the United States with 27 million.[[6]](#cite_note-:4-6) Other markets were Canada (1.6 million by 2014),[[20]](#cite_note-FFVsCanada-20) and Sweden (243,100 through December 2014).[[21]](#cite_note-BAFF-21)[[22]](#cite_note-SwedenSales2012-22)[[23]](#cite_note-SwedenSales2014-23) The Brazilian flex fuel fleet includes over 4 million flexible-fuel motorcycles produced since 2009 through March 2015.[[24]](#cite_note-MotoFlex4mi-24) In Brazil, 65% of flex-fuel car owners were using ethanol fuel regularly in 2009,[[25]](#cite_note-25) while, the actual number of American FFVs being run on [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85") is much lower; surveys conducted in the U.S. have found that 68% of American flex-fuel car owners were not aware they owned an E85 flex.[[18]](#cite_note-SusEthanol-18)

There have been claims that American automakers are motivated to produce flex-fuel vehicles due to a [loophole](/wiki/Loophole "Loophole") in the [Corporate Average Fuel Economy](/wiki/Corporate_Average_Fuel_Economy "Corporate Average Fuel Economy") (CAFE) requirements, which gives the automaker a "fuel economy credit" for every flex-fuel vehicle sold, whether or not the vehicle is actually fueled with E85 in regular use.[[26]](#cite_note-Apollo-26) This loophole allegedly allows the U.S. auto industry to meet CAFE fuel economy targets not by developing more fuel-efficient models, but by spending between US$100 and US$200 extra per vehicle to produce a certain number of flex-fuel models, enabling them to continue selling less fuel-efficient vehicles such as [SUVs](/wiki/Sport_Utility_Vehicle "Sport Utility Vehicle"), which netted higher profit margins than smaller, more fuel-efficient cars.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-Economist-28)

[General Motors EV1](/wiki/General_Motors_EV1 "General Motors EV1") [electric car](/wiki/Electric_car "Electric car")

[Battery electric vehicles](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") (BEVs), also known as all-electric vehicles (AEVs), are electric vehicles whose main energy storage is in the chemical energy of batteries. BEVs are the most common form of what is defined by the [California Air Resources Board](/wiki/California_Air_Resources_Board "California Air Resources Board") (CARB) as [zero emission vehicle](/wiki/Zero_emission_vehicle "Zero emission vehicle") (ZEV) because they produce no tailpipe emissions at the point of operation. The electrical energy carried on board a BEV to power the motors is obtained from a variety of battery chemistries arranged into battery packs. For additional range genset trailers or pusher trailers are sometimes used, forming a type of hybrid vehicle. Batteries used in electric vehicles include "flooded" lead-acid, absorbed glass mat, NiCd, nickel metal hydride, Li-ion, Li-poly and zinc-air batteries.

Attempts at building viable, modern [battery](/wiki/Battery_(electricity) "Battery (electricity)")-powered electric vehicles began in the 1950s with the introduction of the first modern ([transistor](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor") controlled) electric car – the [Henney Kilowatt](/wiki/Henney_Kilowatt "Henney Kilowatt"), even though the concept was out in the market since 1890. Despite the poor sales of the early battery-powered vehicles, development of various battery-powered vehicles continued through the mid-1990s, with such models as the [General Motors EV1](/wiki/General_Motors_EV1 "General Motors EV1") and the [Toyota RAV4 EV](/wiki/Toyota_RAV4_EV "Toyota RAV4 EV").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/2018_Nissan_Leaf_Tekna_Front.jpg/250px-2018_Nissan_Leaf_Tekna_Front.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_Nissan_Leaf_Tekna_Front.jpg)

The [Nissan Leaf](/wiki/Nissan_Leaf "Nissan Leaf") was the world's top selling highway-capable all-electric car until December 2019.[[29]](#cite_note-Model3TopEV-29)

Battery powered cars had primarily used [lead-acid batteries](/wiki/Lead-acid_batteries "Lead-acid batteries") and [NiMH batteries](/wiki/Nickel_metal_hydride "Nickel metal hydride"). Lead-acid batteries' recharge capacity is considerably reduced if they're discharged beyond 75% on a regular basis, making them a less-than-ideal solution. NiMH batteries are a better choice[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], but are considerably more expensive than lead-acid. [Lithium-ion battery](/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery "Lithium-ion battery") powered vehicles such as the [Venturi Fetish](/wiki/Venturi_Fetish "Venturi Fetish") and the [Tesla Roadster](/wiki/Tesla_Roadster_(2008) "Tesla Roadster (2008)") have recently demonstrated excellent performance and range, and nevertheless is used in most mass production models launched since December 2010.

Expanding on traditional lithium-ion batteries predominately used in today's battery electric vehicles, is an emerging science that is paving the way to utilize a carbon fiber structure (a vehicle body or chassis in this case) as a [structural battery](/wiki/Structural_battery "Structural battery"). Experiments being conducted at the [Chalmers University of Technology](https://www.chalmers.se/en/departments/ims/news/Pages/carbon-fibre-can-store-energy.aspx) in Sweden are showing that when coupled with Lithium-ion insertion mechanisms, an enhanced carbon fiber structure can have electromechanical properties. This means that the carbon fiber structure itself can act as its own battery/power source for propulsion. This would negate the need for traditional heavy battery banks, reducing weight and therefore increasing fuel efficiency.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

#### Plug-in hybrid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Plug-in hybrid")]

Main article: [Plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Mitsu_Outlander_PHEV_Oslo_10_2018_3731.jpg/250px-Mitsu_Outlander_PHEV_Oslo_10_2018_3731.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mitsu_Outlander_PHEV_Oslo_10_2018_3731.jpg)

The [Mitsubishi Outlander P-HEV](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Outlander_P-HEV "Mitsubishi Outlander P-HEV") is the world's all-time best selling [plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") with 270,000 units sold through December 2020.[[34]](#cite_note-MOPHEV270k-34)

Plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs) use batteries to power an electric motor, as well as another fuel, such as gasoline or diesel, to power an internal combustion engine or other propulsion source. PHEVs can charge their batteries through charging equipment and [regenerative braking](/wiki/Regenerative_braking "Regenerative braking"). Using electricity from the grid to run the vehicle some or all of the time reduces operating costs and fuel use, relative to conventional vehicles.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Until 2010 most [plug-in hybrids](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") on the road in the U.S. were conversions of conventional hybrid electric vehicles,[[36]](#cite_note-Boschert06-36) and the most prominent PHEVs were conversions of 2004 or later Toyota Prius, which have had plug-in charging and more batteries added and their electric-only range extended.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Chinese battery manufacturer and automaker [BYD Auto](/wiki/BYD_Auto "BYD Auto") released the [F3DM](/wiki/BYD_F3DM "BYD F3DM") to the Chinese fleet market in December 2008[[38]](#cite_note-F3DM-38)[[39]](#cite_note-bwf3dm-39)[[40]](#cite_note-GCC1208-40) and began sales to the general public in [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") in March 2010.[[41]](#cite_note-GCC0310-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Edmunds310-42) [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") began deliveries of the [Chevrolet Volt](/wiki/Chevrolet_Volt "Chevrolet Volt") in the U.S. in December 2010.[[43]](#cite_note-Volt1stDelivery-43) Deliveries to retail customers of the [Fisker Karma](/wiki/Fisker_Karma "Fisker Karma") began in the U.S. in November 2011.

During 2012, the [Toyota Prius Plug-in Hybrid](/wiki/Toyota_Prius_Plug-in_Hybrid "Toyota Prius Plug-in Hybrid"), [Ford C-Max Energi](/wiki/Ford_C-Max_Energi "Ford C-Max Energi"), and [Volvo V60 Plug-in Hybrid](/wiki/Volvo_V60_Plug-in_Hybrid "Volvo V60 Plug-in Hybrid") were released. The following models were launched during 2013 and 2015: [Honda Accord Plug-in Hybrid](/wiki/Honda_Accord_Plug-in_Hybrid "Honda Accord Plug-in Hybrid"), [Mitsubishi Outlander P-HEV](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Outlander_P-HEV "Mitsubishi Outlander P-HEV"), [Ford Fusion Energi](/wiki/Ford_Fusion_Energi "Ford Fusion Energi"), [McLaren P1](/wiki/McLaren_P1 "McLaren P1") (limited edition), [Porsche Panamera S E-Hybrid](/wiki/Porsche_Panamera_S_E-Hybrid "Porsche Panamera S E-Hybrid"), [BYD Qin](/wiki/BYD_Qin "BYD Qin"), [Cadillac ELR](/wiki/Cadillac_ELR "Cadillac ELR"), [BMW i3 REx](/wiki/BMW_i3_REx "BMW i3 REx"), [BMW i8](/wiki/BMW_i8 "BMW i8"), [Porsche 918 Spyder](/wiki/Porsche_918_Spyder "Porsche 918 Spyder") (limited production), [Volkswagen XL1](/wiki/Volkswagen_XL1 "Volkswagen XL1") (limited production), [Audi A3 Sportback e-tron](/wiki/Audi_A3_Sportback_e-tron "Audi A3 Sportback e-tron"), [Volkswagen Golf GTE](/wiki/Volkswagen_Golf_GTE "Volkswagen Golf GTE"), [Mercedes-Benz S 500 e](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_S_500_e "Mercedes-Benz S 500 e"), [Porsche Cayenne S E-Hybrid](/wiki/Porsche_Cayenne_S_E-Hybrid "Porsche Cayenne S E-Hybrid"), [Mercedes-Benz C 350 e](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_C-Class_(W205)#C350_e_Plug-in_Hybrid_(2015-) "Mercedes-Benz C-Class (W205)"), [BYD Tang](/wiki/BYD_Tang "BYD Tang"), [Volkswagen Passat GTE](/wiki/Volkswagen_Passat_GTE "Volkswagen Passat GTE"), [Volvo XC90 T8](/wiki/Volvo_XC90_T8 "Volvo XC90 T8"), [BMW X5 xDrive40e](/wiki/BMW_X5_xDrive40e "BMW X5 xDrive40e"), [Hyundai Sonata PHEV](/wiki/Hyundai_Sonata_PHEV "Hyundai Sonata PHEV"), and [Volvo S60L PHEV](/wiki/Volvo_S60L_PHEV "Volvo S60L PHEV").

As of December 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit), about 500,000 highway-capable plug-in hybrid electric cars had been sold worldwide since December 2008, out of total cumulative global sales of 1.2 million light-duty [plug-in electric vehicles](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicle "Plug-in electric vehicle").[[44]](#cite_note-TopSix2015-44) As of December 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit), the [Volt/Ampera](/wiki/Chevrolet_Volt "Chevrolet Volt") family of [plug-in hybrids](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid"), with combined sales of about 134,500 units is the top selling plug-in hybrid in the world. Ranking next are the [Mitsubishi Outlander P-HEV](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Outlander_P-HEV "Mitsubishi Outlander P-HEV") with about 119,500, and the [Toyota Prius Plug-in Hybrid](/wiki/Toyota_Prius_Plug-in_Hybrid "Toyota Prius Plug-in Hybrid") with almost 78,000.[[1]](#cite_note-Top10PEVs2016-1)

#### Bioalcohol and ethanol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Bioalcohol and ethanol")]

See also: [Alcohol fuel](/wiki/Alcohol_fuel "Alcohol fuel"), [Ethanol fuel](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel"), [Methanol economy](/wiki/Methanol_economy "Methanol economy"), [Methanol fuel](/wiki/Methanol_fuel "Methanol fuel"), [Common ethanol fuel mixtures](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures "Common ethanol fuel mixtures"), [Flexible-fuel vehicle](/wiki/Flexible-fuel_vehicle "Flexible-fuel vehicle"), [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85"), and [Biobutanol](/wiki/Biobutanol "Biobutanol")

The first commercial vehicle that used [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel") as a fuel was the [Ford Model T](/wiki/Ford_Model_T "Ford Model T"), produced from 1908 through 1927. It was fitted with a [carburetor](/wiki/Carburetor "Carburetor") with adjustable jetting, allowing use of gasoline or ethanol, or a combination of both.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Other car manufactures also provided engines for ethanol fuel use.[[18]](#cite_note-SusEthanol-18) In the United States, alcohol fuel was produced in corn-alcohol [stills](/wiki/Still "Still") until [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition "Prohibition") criminalized the production of alcohol in 1919. The use of alcohol as a fuel for [internal combustion engines](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), either alone or in combination with other fuels, lapsed until the [oil price shocks](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") of the 1970s. Furthermore, additional attention was gained because of its possible environmental and long-term economical advantages over fossil fuel.

Both [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel") and [methanol](/wiki/Methanol_fuel "Methanol fuel") have been used as an automotive fuel.[[48]](#cite_note-Methanolstory-48) While both can be obtained from petroleum or natural gas, ethanol has attracted more attention because it is considered a [renewable resource](/wiki/Renewable_resource "Renewable resource"), easily obtained from sugar or [starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch") in crops and other agricultural produce such as [grain](/wiki/Grain "Grain"), [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane"), sugar beets or even [lactose](/wiki/Lactose "Lactose"). Since ethanol occurs in nature whenever yeast happens to find a sugar solution such as overripe fruit, most organisms have evolved some tolerance to [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), whereas [methanol](/wiki/Methanol "Methanol") is toxic. Other experiments involve [butanol](/wiki/Butanol_fuel "Butanol fuel"), which can also be produced by fermentation of plants. Support for ethanol comes from the fact that it is a biomass fuel, which addresses [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions"), though these benefits are now highly debated,[[18]](#cite_note-SusEthanol-18)[[49]](#cite_note-Oxfam-49)[[50]](#cite_note-SearchingerHeimlich2008-50)[[51]](#cite_note-FargioneHill2008-51) including the heated 2008 [food vs fuel](/wiki/Food_vs_fuel "Food vs fuel") debate.

Most modern cars are designed to run on gasoline are capable of running with a blend from 10% up to 15% ethanol mixed into gasoline ([E10-E15](/wiki/E15_fuel "E15 fuel")). With a small amount of redesign, gasoline-powered vehicles can run on ethanol concentrations as high as 85% ([E85](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures#E85 "Common ethanol fuel mixtures")), the maximum set in the United States and Europe due to cold weather during the winter,[[52]](#cite_note-E70green-52) or up to 100% ([E100](/wiki/Neat_alcohol_fuel "Neat alcohol fuel")) in Brazil, with a warmer climate. Ethanol has close to 34% less energy per volume than gasoline,[[53]](#cite_note-EEREFAQ-53)[[54]](#cite_note-EIAATTF-54) consequently fuel economy ratings with ethanol blends are significantly lower than with pure gasoline, but this lower energy content does not translate directly into a 34% reduction in mileage, because there are many other variables that affect the performance of a particular fuel in a particular engine, and also because ethanol has a higher octane rating which is beneficial to high compression ratio engines.

For this reason, for pure or high ethanol blends to be attractive for users, its price must be lower than gasoline to offset the lower fuel economy. As a [rule of thumb](/wiki/Rule_of_thumb "Rule of thumb"), Brazilian consumers are frequently advised by the local media to use more alcohol than gasoline in their mix only when ethanol prices are 30% lower or more than gasoline, as ethanol price fluctuates heavily depending on the results and seasonal harvests of sugar cane and by region.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) In the US, and based on EPA tests for all 2006 [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85") models, the average fuel economy for E85 vehicles was found 25.56% lower than unleaded gasoline.[[18]](#cite_note-SusEthanol-18) The EPA-rated mileage of current American flex-fuel vehicles[[57]](#cite_note-57) could be considered when making price comparisons, though E85 has octane rating of about 104 and could be used as a substitute for premium gasoline. Regional retail E85 prices vary widely across the US, with more favorable prices in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest") region, where most corn is grown and ethanol produced. In August 2008 the US average spread between the price of [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85") and gasoline was 16.9%, while in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") was 35%, 30% in [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), 19% in [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), 12 to 15% in California, and just 3% in [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah").[[58]](#cite_note-PricesUS08-58) Depending on the vehicle capabilities, the break even price of E85 usually has to be between 25 and 30% lower than gasoline.[[18]](#cite_note-SusEthanol-18)

Reacting to the high price of oil and its growing dependence on imports, in 1975 [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") launched the [Pro-alcool program](/wiki/Neat_ethanol_vehicle "Neat ethanol vehicle"), a huge government-subsidized effort to manufacture ethanol fuel (from its sugar cane crop) and ethanol-powered automobiles. These ethanol-only vehicles were very popular in the 1980s, but became economically impractical when oil prices fell – and sugar prices rose – late in that decade. In May 2003 [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen") built for the first time a commercial ethanol [flexible fuel car](/wiki/Flexible-fuel_vehicle "Flexible-fuel vehicle"), the [Gol](/wiki/Volkswagen_Gol "Volkswagen Gol") 1.6 Total Flex. These vehicles were a commercial success and by early 2009 nine other Brazilian manufacturers are producing flexible fuel vehicles: [Chevrolet](/wiki/Chevrolet "Chevrolet"), [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat"), [Ford](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), [Peugeot](/wiki/Peugeot "Peugeot"), [Renault](/wiki/Renault "Renault"), [Honda](/wiki/Honda "Honda"), [Mitsubishi](/wiki/Mitsubishi "Mitsubishi"), [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota"), [Citroën](/wiki/Citro%C3%ABn "Citroën"), and [Nissan](/wiki/Nissan "Nissan").[[13]](#cite_note-ANFAVEA2010-13)[[59]](#cite_note-59) The adoption of the flex technology was so rapid, that flexible fuel cars reached 87.6% of new car sales in July 2008.[[60]](#cite_note-July2008-60) As of August 2008, the fleet of "flex" automobiles and light commercial vehicles had reached 6 million new vehicles sold,[[61]](#cite_note-Folha_Online-61) representing almost 19% of all registered light vehicles.[[62]](#cite_note-DENATRAN-62) The rapid success of "flex" vehicles, as they are popularly known, was made possible by the existence of 33,000 filling stations with at least one ethanol pump available by 2006, a heritage of the *Pro-alcool* program.[[63]](#cite_note-Wilson-63)[[26]](#cite_note-Apollo-26)

In the United States, initial support to develop alternative fuels by the government was also a response to the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"), and later on, as a goal to improve air quality. Also, liquid fuels were preferred over gaseous fuels not only because they have a better volumetric energy density but also because they were the most compatible fuels with existing distribution systems and engines, thus avoiding a big departure from the existing technologies and taking advantage of the vehicle and the refueling infrastructure.[[48]](#cite_note-Methanolstory-48) California led the search of sustainable alternatives with interest in [methanol](/wiki/Methanol_fuel "Methanol fuel").[[48]](#cite_note-Methanolstory-48) In 1996, a new FFV [Ford Taurus](/wiki/Ford_Taurus "Ford Taurus") was developed, with models fully capable of running either methanol or ethanol blended with gasoline.[[48]](#cite_note-Methanolstory-48)[[64]](#cite_note-64) This ethanol version of the Taurus was the first commercial production of an E85 FFV.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The momentum of the FFV production programs at the American car companies continued, although by the end of the 1990s, the emphasis was on the FFV E85 version, as it is today.[[48]](#cite_note-Methanolstory-48) Ethanol was preferred over methanol because there is a large support in the farming community and thanks to government's incentive programs and corn-based ethanol subsidies.[[66]](#cite_note-66) [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") also tested both the M85 and the E85 flexifuel vehicles, but due to agriculture policy, in the end emphasis was given to the ethanol flexifuel vehicles.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Bus running on soybean biodiesel

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Biodiesel_B20_pump_DCA_07_2010_9832.jpg/250px-Biodiesel_B20_pump_DCA_07_2010_9832.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biodiesel_B20_pump_DCA_07_2010_9832.jpg)

[Biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") [(B20)](/wiki/B20_(biodiesel) "B20 (biodiesel)") pump in the U.S.

The main benefit of diesel combustion engines is that they have a 44% fuel burn efficiency; compared with just 25–30% in the best gasoline engines.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[*[circular reference](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Wikipedia_and_sources_that_mirror_or_use_it "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In addition diesel fuel has slightly higher [energy density](/wiki/Energy_density "Energy density") by volume than gasoline. This makes diesel engines capable of achieving much better fuel economy than gasoline vehicles.

[Biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") (fatty acid methyl ester), is commercially available in most oilseed-producing states in the United States. As of 2005, it is somewhat more expensive than fossil diesel, though it is still commonly produced in relatively small quantities (in comparison to petroleum products and ethanol). Many farmers who raise oilseeds use a biodiesel blend in tractors and equipment as a matter of policy, to foster production of biodiesel and raise public awareness. It is sometimes easier to find biodiesel in rural areas than in cities. Biodiesel has lower [energy density](/wiki/Energy_density "Energy density") than fossil diesel fuel, so biodiesel vehicles are not quite able to keep up with the fuel economy of a fossil fuelled diesel vehicle, if the diesel injection system is not reset for the new fuel. If the injection timing is changed to take account of the higher cetane value of biodiesel, the difference in economy is negligible. Because biodiesel contains more oxygen than diesel or [vegetable oil fuel](/wiki/Vegetable_oil_fuel "Vegetable oil fuel"), it produces the lowest emissions from diesel engines, and is lower in most emissions than gasoline engines. Biodiesel has a higher lubricity than mineral diesel and is an additive in European pump diesel for lubricity and emissions reduction.

Some [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_fuel "Diesel fuel")-powered cars can run with minor modifications on 100% pure [vegetable oils](/wiki/Vegetable_oil "Vegetable oil"). Vegetable oils tend to thicken (or solidify if it is waste cooking oil), in cold weather conditions so vehicle modifications (a two tank system with diesel start/stop tank), are essential in order to heat the fuel prior to use under most circumstances. Heating to the temperature of engine coolant reduces fuel viscosity, to the range cited by injection system manufacturers, for systems prior to 'common rail' or 'unit injection ( VW PD)' systems. Waste vegetable oil, especially if it has been used for a long time, may become hydrogenated and have increased acidity. This can cause the thickening of fuel, gumming in the engine and acid damage of the fuel system. Biodiesel does not have this problem, because it is chemically processed to be PH neutral and lower viscosity. Modern low emission diesels (most often Euro -3 and -4 compliant), typical of the current production in the European industry, would require extensive modification of injector system, pumps and seals etc. due to the higher operating pressures, that are designed thinner (heated) mineral diesel than ever before, for atomisation, if they were to use pure vegetable oil as fuel. Vegetable oil fuel is not suitable for these vehicles as they are currently produced. This reduces the market as increasing numbers of new vehicles are not able to use it. However, the German Elsbett company has successfully produced single tank vegetable oil fuel systems for several decades, and has worked with Volkswagen on their TDI engines. This shows that it is technologically possible to use vegetable oil as a fuel in high efficiency / low emission diesel engines.

[Greasestock](/wiki/Greasestock "Greasestock") is an event held yearly in [Yorktown Heights, New York](/wiki/Yorktown_Heights,_New_York "Yorktown Heights, New York"), and is one of the largest showcases of vehicles using waste oil as a biofuel in the United States.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

#### Biogas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Biogas")]

Main article: [Biogas](/wiki/Biogas "Biogas")

Compressed biogas may be used for internal combustion engines after purification of the raw gas. The removal of H2O, H2S and particles can be seen as standard producing a gas which has the same quality as compressed natural gas.

### Compressed natural gas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Compressed natural gas")]

Main article: [Natural gas vehicle](/wiki/Natural_gas_vehicle "Natural gas vehicle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/SAO_09_2008_Fiat_Siena_TetraFuel_2_views_v1.jpg/250px-SAO_09_2008_Fiat_Siena_TetraFuel_2_views_v1.jpg)](/wiki/File:SAO_09_2008_Fiat_Siena_TetraFuel_2_views_v1.jpg)

The Brazilian [Fiat Siena Tetrafuel](/wiki/Fiat_Siena "Fiat Siena") 1.4, the first [multifuel](/wiki/Multifuel "Multifuel") car that runs as a [flexible-fuel](/wiki/Flexible-fuel_vehicle "Flexible-fuel vehicle") on pure gasoline, or [E25](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures#E25 "Common ethanol fuel mixtures"), or [E100](/wiki/Neat_alcohol_fuel "Neat alcohol fuel"); or runs as a [bi-fuel](/wiki/Bi-fuel_vehicle "Bi-fuel vehicle") with [natural gas (CNG)](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas").

High-pressure [compressed natural gas](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas") (CNG), mainly composed of methane, that is used to fuel normal combustion engines instead of gasoline. Combustion of methane produces the least amount of CO2 of all fossil fuels. Gasoline cars can be retrofitted to CNG and become bifuel [Natural gas vehicles](/wiki/Natural_gas_vehicle "Natural gas vehicle") (NGVs) as the gasoline tank is kept. The driver can switch between CNG and gasoline during operation. [Natural gas vehicles](/wiki/Natural_gas_vehicle "Natural gas vehicle") (NGVs) are popular in regions or countries where natural gas is abundant. Widespread use began in the [Po River Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley") of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), and later became very popular in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") by the eighties, though its use has declined.[[73]](#cite_note-TwoBillion-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Metrobus_powered_with_CNG_5198_DCA_03_2009.jpg/250px-Metrobus_powered_with_CNG_5198_DCA_03_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metrobus_powered_with_CNG_5198_DCA_03_2009.jpg)

[Buses](/wiki/Bus "Bus") powered with [CNG](/wiki/Compressed_natural_gas "Compressed natural gas") are common in the United States.

As of 2017, there were 24.5 million [natural gas vehicles](/wiki/Natural_gas_vehicle "Natural gas vehicle") worldwide, led by China (5.35 million) followed by [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") (4.0 million), [India](/wiki/India "India") (3.05 million), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") (3 million), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") (2.3 million), and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") (1.78 million).[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10)

As of 2010, the Asia-Pacific region led the global market with a share of 54%.[[74]](#cite_note-IANGV-74) In Europe they are popular in Italy (730,000), Ukraine (200,000), Armenia (101,352), Russia (100,000) and Germany (91,500),[[74]](#cite_note-IANGV-74) and they are becoming more so as various manufacturers produce factory made cars, buses, vans and heavy vehicles.[[75]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-75) In the United States CNG powered buses are the favorite choice of several [public transit](/wiki/Public_transit "Public transit") agencies, with an estimated CNG bus fleet of some 130,000.[[76]](#cite_note-GreenCar08-76) Other countries where CNG-powered buses are popular include India, Australia, Argentina, and Germany.[[73]](#cite_note-TwoBillion-73)

CNG vehicles are common in South America, where these vehicles are mainly used as [taxicabs](/wiki/Taxicab "Taxicab") in main cities of Argentina and Brazil. Normally, standard gasoline vehicles are retrofitted in specialized shops, which involve installing the gas cylinder in the trunk and the CNG injection system and electronics. The Brazilian GNV fleet is concentrated in the cities of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo").[[77]](#cite_note-77) Pike Research reports that almost 90% of NGVs in Latin America have [bi-fuel engines](/wiki/Bi-fuel_engine "Bi-fuel engine"), allowing these vehicles to run on either gasoline or CNG.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Dual fuel vehicle is referred as the vehicle using two types of fuel in the same time (can be gas + liquid, gas + gas, liquid + liquid) with different fuel tank.

### Hybrid electric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hybrid electric")]

Main article: [Hybrid electric vehicle](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/2016_Toyota_Prius_%28ZVW50L%29_Hybrid_liftback_%282016-04-02%29_01.jpg/250px-2016_Toyota_Prius_%28ZVW50L%29_Hybrid_liftback_%282016-04-02%29_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_Toyota_Prius_(ZVW50L)_Hybrid_liftback_(2016-04-02)_01.jpg)

The [Toyota Prius](/wiki/Toyota_Prius "Toyota Prius") is the world's best-selling [hybrid electric vehicle](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle"), with global sales of almost 4 million units through January 2017.[[80]](#cite_note-TMC10miHEVs-80)

A [hybrid vehicle](/wiki/Hybrid_vehicle "Hybrid vehicle") uses multiple propulsion systems to provide motive power. The most common type of hybrid vehicle is the [gasoline-electric hybrid vehicles](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle"), which use gasoline (petrol) and electric batteries for the energy used to power [internal-combustion engines](/wiki/Internal-combustion_engine "Internal-combustion engine") (ICEs) and electric motors. These motors are usually relatively small and would be considered "underpowered" by themselves, but they can provide a normal driving experience when used in combination during acceleration and other maneuvers that require greater power.

The [Toyota Prius](/wiki/Toyota_Prius "Toyota Prius") first went on sale in Japan in 1997 and it is sold worldwide since 2000.

As of January 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit), there are over 50 models of hybrid electric cars available in several world markets, with more than 12 million hybrid electric vehicles sold worldwide since their inception in 1997.[[80]](#cite_note-TMC10miHEVs-80)[[81]](#cite_note-US4mi-81)

### Hydrogen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hydrogen")]

Main articles: [Hydrogen vehicle](/wiki/Hydrogen_vehicle "Hydrogen vehicle") and [Hydrogen economy](/wiki/Hydrogen_economy "Hydrogen economy")

A [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") car is an automobile which uses hydrogen as its primary source of power for locomotion. These cars generally use the hydrogen in one of two methods: combustion or [fuel-cell](/wiki/Fuel-cell "Fuel-cell") conversion. In combustion, the hydrogen is "burned" in engines in fundamentally the same method as traditional gasoline cars. The common [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), usually fueled with gasoline (petrol) or diesel liquids, can be converted to run on gaseous hydrogen. This emits water at the point of use, and during combustion with air [NOx](/wiki/NOx "NOx") can be produced.[[82]](#cite_note-82) However, the most efficient use of hydrogen involves the use of [fuel cells](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell") and [electric motors](/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor") instead of a traditional engine. Hydrogen reacts with [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") inside the fuel cells, which produces electricity to power the motors, with the only byproduct from the spent hydrogen being water.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

A small number of commercially available [hydrogen fuel cell cars](/wiki/List_of_fuel_cell_vehicles "List of fuel cell vehicles") currently exist: the [Hyundai NEXO](/wiki/Hyundai_Nexo "Hyundai Nexo"), [Toytota Mirai](/wiki/Toyota_Mirai "Toyota Mirai"), and previously the [Honda FCX Clarity](/wiki/Honda_FCX_Clarity "Honda FCX Clarity"). One primary area of research is [hydrogen storage](/wiki/Hydrogen_storage "Hydrogen storage"), to try to increase the range of hydrogen vehicles while reducing the weight, [energy consumption](/wiki/Energy_consumption "Energy consumption"), and complexity of the storage systems. Two primary methods of storage are metal hydrides and compression. Some believe that hydrogen cars will never be economically viable and that the emphasis on this technology is a diversion from the development and popularization of more efficient [battery electric vehicles](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle").[[84]](#cite_note-Collins-84)

In the light road vehicle segment, by the end of 2022, 70,200 hydrogen fuel cell electric vehicles had been sold worldwide,[[85]](#cite_note-85) compared with 26 million plug-in electric vehicles.[[86]](#cite_note-Outlook2023-86) With the rapid rise of [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") and associated battery technology and infrastructure, the global scope for hydrogen's role in cars is shrinking relative to earlier expectations.[[84]](#cite_note-Collins-84)[[87]](#cite_note-role-87)

### Electric, fed by external source

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Electric, fed by external source")]

Electric power fed from an external source to the vehicle is standard in [railway electrification](/wiki/Railway_electrification "Railway electrification"). At such systems usually the tracks form one pole, while the other is usually a single overhead wire or a rail insulated against ground.

On roads this system does not work as described, as normal road surfaces are very poor electric conductors; and so electric vehicles fed with external power on roads require at least two overhead wires. The most common type of road vehicles fed with electricity from external source are [trolleybusses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus"), but there are also some trucks powered with this technology. The advantage is that the vehicle can be operated without breaks for refueling or charging. Disadvantages include: a large infrastructure of electric wires; difficulty in driving as one has to prevent a dewirement of the vehicle; vehicles cannot overtake each other; a danger of electrocution; and an aesthetic problem.

Wireless transmission (see [Wireless power transfer](/wiki/Wireless_power_transfer "Wireless power transfer")) is possible, in principle; but the infrastructure (especially wiring) necessary for inductive or capacitive coupling would be extensive and expensive. In principle it is also possible to transmit energy by microwaves or by lasers to the vehicle, but this may be inefficient and dangerous for the power required. Beside this, in the case of lasers one requires a guidance system to track the vehicle to be powered, as laser beams have a small diameter.

## Comparative assessment of fossil and alternative fuels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Comparative assessment of fossil and alternative fuels")]

[![Comparative assessments of well-to-wheel greenhouse gas emissions per km driven for conventional and battery-electric vehicles, showing battery-electric vehicles performing best in four major jurisdictions including those that generate electricity from coal.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/ICCT_lifecycle_ICE_and_EV_emissions_2021_-_2023.png/960px-ICCT_lifecycle_ICE_and_EV_emissions_2021_-_2023.png)](/wiki/File:ICCT_lifecycle_ICE_and_EV_emissions_2021_-_2023.png)

Even in countries where electricity is largely generated from coal, such as China and India, battery-electric vehicles (BEVs) have lower lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions. The advantages of BEVs will further increase by 2030 as countries increasingly adopt clean electricity sources.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7): ii

[![Chart comparing lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions for various vehicle types](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/IEA_average_lifetime_emissions_for_vehicles_sold_in_2019.png/960px-IEA_average_lifetime_emissions_for_vehicles_sold_in_2019.png)](/wiki/File:IEA_average_lifetime_emissions_for_vehicles_sold_in_2019.png)

Battery electric vehicles have lower lifecycle emissions than other vehicle types. Abbreviations used in this chart: ICE(V): internal combustion engine vehicle; CNG: compressed natural gas; HEV: hybrid electric vehicle; BEV: battery electric vehicle; PHEV: plugin hybrid electric vehicle; FCEV: [fuel cell](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell") vehicle; STEPS: IEA's [Stated Policies Scenario](/wiki/International_Energy_Agency#Scenarios "International Energy Agency"); APS: IEA's [Announced Pledges Scenario](/wiki/International_Energy_Agency#Scenarios "International Energy Agency"); NZE: IEA's [Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario](/wiki/International_Energy_Agency#Scenarios "International Energy Agency").[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

Comparative assessments of conventional fossil and alternative fuel vehicles usually encompass more than in-use environmental impacts and running costs. They factor in issues like resource extractive impacts (e.g. for battery manufacture or fossil fuel extraction), 'well-to-wheel' efficiency, and the [carbon intensity](/wiki/Carbon_intensity "Carbon intensity") of electricity in different geographies.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7): 3–9 In general, the lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions of [battery-electric vehicles](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") are lower than emissions from hydrogen, PHEV, hybrid, compressed natural gas, gasoline, and diesel vehicles.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) BEVs have lower emissions than internal combustion engine vehicles even in places where electricity generation is relatively [carbon-intensive](/wiki/Carbon-intensive "Carbon-intensive"), for example China where electricity is predominantly generated from coal.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

The [Peugeot](/wiki/Peugeot "Peugeot") 2008 HYbrid air prototype replaced conventional hybrid batteries with a compressed air propulsion system.

The air engine is an emission-free piston engine that uses compressed air as a source of energy. The first compressed air car was invented by a French engineer named [Guy Nègre](/wiki/Guy_N%C3%A8gre "Guy Nègre"). The expansion of compressed air may be used to drive the pistons in a modified piston engine. Efficiency of operation is gained through the use of environmental heat at normal temperature to warm the otherwise cold expanded air from the storage tank. This non-adiabatic expansion has the potential to greatly increase the efficiency of the machine. The only exhaust is cold air (−15 °C), which could also be used to air condition the car. The source for air is a pressurized carbon-fiber tank. Air is delivered to the engine via a rather conventional injection system. Unique crank design within the engine increases the time during which the air charge is warmed from ambient sources and a two-stage process allows improved heat transfer rates.

### Electric, stored-otherway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Electric, stored-otherway")]

Electricity can be also stored in supercapacitors and superconductors. However superconductor storage is unsuitable for vehicle propulsion as it requires extremely low temperatures and produces strong magnetic fields. Supercapacitors, however, can be used in vehicles and are used in some trams on sections without overhead wire. They can be charged during regular stops, while passengers enter and leave the train, but can only travel a few kilometres with the stored energy. However, this is no problem in this case as the next stop is usually in reachable distance.

[Nuna](/wiki/Nuna "Nuna") team at a racecourse.

A solar car is an electric vehicle powered by solar energy obtained from solar panels on the car. Solar panels cannot currently be used to directly supply a car with a suitable amount of power at this time, but they can be used to extend the range of electric vehicles. As of 2022, a handful of solar electric cars with varying performance are becoming commercially available, from [Fisker](/wiki/Fisker_Inc. "Fisker Inc.") and [Lightyear](/wiki/Lightyear_0 "Lightyear 0"), among others.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Solar cars are raced in competitions such as the World Solar Challenge and the North American Solar Challenge. These events are often sponsored by Government agencies such as the United States Department of Energy keen to promote the development of [alternative energy](/wiki/Alternative_energy "Alternative energy") technology such as solar cells and electric vehicles. Such challenges are often entered by universities to develop their students' engineering and technological skills as well as motor vehicle manufacturers such as GM and Honda.

### Dimethyl ether fuel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Dimethyl ether fuel")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Chemrec_5_-_small.JPG/250px-Chemrec_5_-_small.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chemrec_5_-_small.JPG)

Installation of BioDME synthesis towers at Chemrec's pilot facility

[Dimethyl ether](/wiki/Dimethyl_ether "Dimethyl ether") (DME) is a promising fuel in [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engines "Diesel engines"),[[91]](#cite_note-91) [petrol engines](/wiki/Petrol_engine "Petrol engine") (30% DME / 70% LPG), and [gas turbines](/wiki/Gas_turbines "Gas turbines") owing to its high [cetane number](/wiki/Cetane_number "Cetane number"), which is 55, compared to diesel's, which is 40–53.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-SemelsbergerBorup2006-93) Only moderate modifications are needed to convert a diesel engine to burn DME. The simplicity of this short carbon chain compound leads during combustion to very low emissions of particulate matter, NOx, CO. For these reasons as well as being sulfur-free, DME meets even the most stringent emission regulations in Europe (EURO5), U.S. (U.S. 2010), and Japan (2009 Japan).[[94]](#cite_note-94) [Mobil](/wiki/Mobil "Mobil") is using DME in their [methanol to gasoline](/wiki/Gas_to_liquids "Gas to liquids") process.

DME is being developed as a synthetic [second generation biofuel](/wiki/Second_generation_biofuel "Second generation biofuel") (BioDME), which can be manufactured from [lignocellulosic biomass](/wiki/Lignocellulosic_biomass "Lignocellulosic biomass").[[95]](#cite_note-95) In 2006 the EU considered BioDME in its potential biofuel mix in 2030;[[96]](#cite_note-96) the [Volvo](/wiki/Volvo "Volvo") Group was the coordinator for the [European Community](/wiki/European_Community "European Community") [Seventh Framework Programme](/wiki/Seventh_Framework_Programme "Seventh Framework Programme") project BioDME[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) where [Chemrec's](/wiki/Chemrec "Chemrec") BioDME pilot plant based on [black liquor](/wiki/Black_liquor "Black liquor") [gasification](/wiki/Gasification "Gasification") is nearing completion in [Piteå](/wiki/Pite%C3%A5 "Piteå"), Sweden.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

### Ammonia fuelled vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Ammonia fuelled vehicles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/AmmoniacalGasEngineStreetcarARWaud.jpeg/250px-AmmoniacalGasEngineStreetcarARWaud.jpeg)](/wiki/File:AmmoniacalGasEngineStreetcarARWaud.jpeg)

Ammoniacal gas engine [streetcar in New Orleans](/wiki/Streetcars_in_New_Orleans "Streetcars in New Orleans") drawn by [Alfred Waud](/wiki/Alfred_Waud "Alfred Waud") in 1871

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/X-15.jpg/250px-X-15.jpg)](/wiki/File:X-15.jpg)

The [X-15](/wiki/X-15 "X-15") aircraft used ammonia as one component [fuel](/wiki/Fuel "Fuel") of its [rocket](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket") engine.

[Ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia_production "Ammonia production") is produced by combining gaseous hydrogen with nitrogen from the air. Large-scale ammonia production uses natural gas for the source of hydrogen. Ammonia was used during World War II to power buses in Belgium, and in engine and solar energy applications prior to 1900. Liquid ammonia also fuelled the [Reaction Motors XLR99](/wiki/Reaction_Motors_XLR99 "Reaction Motors XLR99") rocket engine, that powered the [X-15](/wiki/X-15 "X-15") hypersonic research aircraft. Although not as powerful as other fuels, it left no soot in the reusable rocket engine and its density approximately matches the density of the oxidizer, liquid oxygen, which simplified the aircraft's design.

[Ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia") has been proposed as a practical alternative to [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") for [internal combustion engines](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engines "Internal combustion engines").[[100]](#cite_note-c1-100) The calorific value of ammonia is 22.5 MJ/kg (9690 [BTU](/wiki/BTU "BTU")/lb), which is about half that of diesel. In a normal engine, in which the water vapour is not condensed, the calorific value of ammonia will be about 21% less than this figure. It can be used in existing engines with only minor modifications to [carburettors](/wiki/Carburettor "Carburettor")/[injectors](/wiki/Injector "Injector").

When ammonia is produced using coal, the CO2 emitted has the potential to be sequestered[[100]](#cite_note-c1-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101) (the combustion products are nitrogen and water).

Ammonia engines or ammonia motors, using ammonia as a [working fluid](/wiki/Working_fluid "Working fluid"), have been proposed and occasionally used.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The principle is similar to that used in a [fireless locomotive](/wiki/Fireless_locomotive "Fireless locomotive"), but with ammonia as the working fluid, instead of steam or compressed air. Ammonia engines were used experimentally in the 19th century by [Goldsworthy Gurney](/wiki/Goldsworthy_Gurney "Goldsworthy Gurney") in the UK and in [streetcars in New Orleans](/wiki/Streetcars_in_New_Orleans "Streetcars in New Orleans"). In 1981 a Canadian company converted a 1981 Chevrolet Impala to operate using ammonia as fuel.[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)

Ammonia and GreenNH3 is being used with success by developers in Canada,[[105]](#cite_note-105) since it can run in spark ignited or diesel engines with minor modifications, also the only green fuel to power jet engines, and despite its toxicity is reckoned to be no more dangerous than petrol or LPG.[[106]](#cite_note-claverton-energy.com-106) It can be made from renewable electricity, and having half the density of petrol or diesel can be readily carried in sufficient quantities in vehicles. On complete combustion it has no emissions other than nitrogen and water vapour. The combustion chemical formula is 4 NH3 + 3 O2 → 2 N2 + 6 H2O, 75% water is the result.

### Charcoal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Charcoal")]

In the 1930s [Tang Zhongming](/wiki/Tang_Zhongming "Tang Zhongming") made an invention using abundant [charcoal](/wiki/Charcoal "Charcoal") resources for Chinese auto market. The charcoal-fuelled car was later used intensively in China, serving the army and conveyancer after the outbreak of World War II.

### Liquefied natural gas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Liquefied natural gas")]

Main article: [Natural gas vehicle](/wiki/Natural_gas_vehicle "Natural gas vehicle")

[Liquefied natural gas](/wiki/Liquefied_natural_gas "Liquefied natural gas") (LNG) is natural gas that has been cooled to a point at which it becomes a [cryogenic](/wiki/Cryogenic "Cryogenic") liquid. In this liquid state, natural gas is more than 2 times as dense as highly compressed CNG. LNG fuel systems function on any vehicle capable of burning natural gas. Unlike CNG, which is stored at high pressure (typically 3000 or 3600 psi) and then regulated to a lower pressure that the engine can accept, LNG is stored at low pressure (50 to 150 psi) and simply vaporized by a heat exchanger before entering the fuel metering devices to the engine. Because of its high energy density compared to CNG, it is very suitable for those interested in long ranges while running on natural gas.

In the United States, the LNG supply chain is the main thing that has held back this fuel source from growing rapidly. The LNG supply chain is very analogous to that of diesel or gasoline. First, pipeline natural gas is liquefied in large quantities, which is analogous to refining gasoline or diesel. Then, the LNG is transported via semi trailer to fuel stations where it is stored in bulk tanks until it is dispensed into a vehicle. CNG, on the other hand, requires expensive compression at each station to fill the high-pressure cylinder cascades.

### Autogas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Autogas")]

Main article: [Autogas](/wiki/Autogas "Autogas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Propane_powered_blue_bird_vision.jpg/250px-Propane_powered_blue_bird_vision.jpg)](/wiki/File:Propane_powered_blue_bird_vision.jpg)

A propane-fueled [school bus](/wiki/School_bus "School bus") in the United States

LPG or [liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas") (LPG) is a low pressure liquefied gas mixture composed mainly of propane and butane which burns in conventional gasoline combustion engines with less CO2 than gasoline. Gasoline cars can be retrofitted to LPG aka Autogas and become bifuel vehicles as the gasoline tank is not removed, allowing drivers to switch between LPG and gasoline during operation. Estimated 10 million vehicles running worldwide.

There are 24.9 million [LPG powered vehicles](/wiki/Autogas "Autogas") worldwide as of December 2013, led by [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") with 3.93 million, [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") (2.4 million), and Poland (2.75 million).[[9]](#cite_note-LPG-9) In the U.S., 190,000 on-road vehicles use propane,[[107]](#cite_note-107) and 450,000 forklifts use it for power. However, it is banned in Pakistan (DEC 2013) as it is considered a risk to public safety by OGRA.

### Formic acid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Formic acid")]

Main article: [Formic acid](/wiki/Formic_acid "Formic acid")

Formic acid is used by converting it first to hydrogen, and using that in a hydrogen [fuel cell](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell"). It can also be used directly in [formic acid fuel cells](/wiki/Formic_acid_fuel_cell "Formic acid fuel cell"). Formic acid is much easier to store than hydrogen.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)

Liquid nitrogen (LN2) is a method of storing energy. Energy is used to liquefy air, and then LN2 is produced by evaporation, and distributed. LN2 is exposed to ambient heat in the car and the resulting nitrogen gas can be used to power a piston or turbine engine. The maximum amount of energy that can be extracted from LN2 is 213 Watt-hours per kg (W·h/kg) or 173 W·h per liter, in which a maximum of 70 W·h/kg can be utilized with an isothermal expansion process. Such a vehicle with a 350-liter (93 gallon) tank can achieve ranges similar to a gasoline powered vehicle with a 50-liter (13 gallon) tank. Theoretical future engines, using cascading topping cycles, can improve this to around 110 W·h/kg with a quasi-isothermal expansion process. The advantages are zero harmful emissions and superior energy densities compared to a [compressed-air vehicle](/wiki/Compressed-air_vehicle "Compressed-air vehicle") as well as being able to refill the tank in a matter of minutes.

### Nuclear power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Nuclear power")]

Main article: [Nuclear propulsion](/wiki/Nuclear_propulsion "Nuclear propulsion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Curiosity_Self-Portrait_at_%27Big_Sky%27_Drilling_Site.jpg/250px-Curiosity_Self-Portrait_at_%27Big_Sky%27_Drilling_Site.jpg)](/wiki/File:Curiosity_Self-Portrait_at_%27Big_Sky%27_Drilling_Site.jpg)

Mars rover *[Curiosity](/wiki/Curiosity_(rover) "Curiosity (rover)")* driven by radioisotope thermoelectric generators

In principle, it is possible to build a vehicle powered by nuclear fission or nuclear decay. However, there are two major problems: first one has to transform the energy, which comes as heat and radiation into energy usable for a drive. One possible would be to use a steam turbine as in a nuclear power plant, but such a device would take too much space. A more suitable way would be direct conversion into electricity for example with thermoelements or thermionic devices. The second problem is that nuclear fission produces high levels of neutron and gamma rays, which require excessive shielding, that would result in a vehicle too large for use on public roads. However studies were made in this way by [Ford Nucleon](/wiki/Ford_Nucleon "Ford Nucleon").

A better way for a nuclear powered vehicle would be the use of power of radioactive decay in [radioisotope thermoelectric generators](/wiki/Radioisotope_thermoelectric_generator "Radioisotope thermoelectric generator"), which are also very safe and reliable. The required shielding of these devices depends on the used radio nuclide. [Plutonium-238](/wiki/Plutonium-238 "Plutonium-238") as nearly pure alpha radiator does not require much shielding. As prices for suitable radionuclide are high and energy density is low (generating 1 watt with plutonium-238 requires a half gram of it), this way of propulsion is too expensive for wide use. Also radioisotope thermoelectric generators offer according to their large content of high radioactive material an extreme danger in case of misuse for example by terrorists. The only vehicle in use, which is driven by radioisotope thermoelectric generators is the Mars rover [Curiosity](/wiki/Curiosity_(rover) "Curiosity (rover)").

Other forms of nuclear power as fusion and annihilation are at present not available for vehicle propulsion, as no working fusion reactor is available and it is questionable if one can ever built one with a size suitable for a road vehicle. Annihilation may perhaps work in some ways (see [antimatter drive](/wiki/Antimatter_drive "Antimatter drive")), but there is no technology existing to produce and store enough antimatter.

### Pedal-assisted electric hybrid vehicle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Pedal-assisted electric hybrid vehicle")]

In very small vehicles, the power demand decreases, so human power can be employed to make a significant improvement in battery life. Three such commercially made vehicles are the [Sinclair C5](/wiki/Sinclair_C5 "Sinclair C5"), ELF and [TWIKE](/wiki/TWIKE "TWIKE").

### Flywheels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Flywheels")]

Flywheels can be also used for alternative fuel and were used in the 1950s for the propulsion of buses in Switzerland, the such called [gyrobuses](/wiki/Gyrobus "Gyrobus"). The flywheel of the bus was loaded up by electric power at the terminals of the line and allowed it to travel a way up to 8 kilometres just with its flywheel. Flywheel-powered vehicles are quieter than vehicles with combustion engine, require no overhead wire and generate no exhausts, but the flywheel device has a great weight (1.5 tons for 5 kWh) and requires special safety measures due to its high rotational speed.

### Silanes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Silanes")]

[Silanes](/wiki/Silanes "Silanes") higher than heptasilane can be stored like gasoline and may also work as fuel. They have the advantage that they can also burn with the nitrogen of the air, but have as major disadvantage its high price and that its combustion products are solid, which gives trouble in combustion engines.

### Spring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Spring")]

The power of wound-up springs or twisted rubber cords can be used for the propulsion of small vehicles. However this way of energy storage allows only saving small energy amounts not suitable for the propulsion of vehicles for transporting people. Spring-powered vehicles are [wind-up toys](/wiki/Wind-up_toy "Wind-up toy") or [mousetrap cars](/wiki/Mousetrap_car "Mousetrap car").

### Steam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Steam")]

Main article: [Steam car](/wiki/Steam_car "Steam car")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Cars-stanleysteamcar-amoswolfe.jpg/250px-Cars-stanleysteamcar-amoswolfe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cars-stanleysteamcar-amoswolfe.jpg)

The [Stanley Steamer car](/wiki/Stanley_Steamer "Stanley Steamer")

A steam car is a car that has a [steam engine](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine"). Wood, coal, [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), or others can be used as [fuel](/wiki/Fuel "Fuel"). The fuel is burned in a [boiler](/wiki/Boiler "Boiler") and the heat converts water into [steam](/wiki/Steam "Steam"). When the water turns to steam, it expands. The expansion creates [pressure](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure"). The pressure pushes the [pistons](/wiki/Piston "Piston") back and forth. This turns the [driveshaft](/wiki/Driveshaft "Driveshaft") to spin the wheels which provides moves the car forward. It works like a coal-fueled [steam train](/wiki/Steam_train "Steam train"), or [steam boat](/wiki/Steam_boat "Steam boat"). The steam car was the next logical step in independent transport.

Steam cars take a long time to start, but some can reach speeds over 100 mph (161 km/h) eventually. The late model [Doble steam cars](/wiki/Doble_steam_car "Doble steam car") could be brought to operational condition in less than 30 seconds, had high top speeds and fast acceleration, but were expensive to buy.

A steam engine uses [external combustion](/wiki/External_combustion "External combustion"), as opposed to internal combustion. Gasoline-powered cars are more efficient at about 25–28% [efficiency](/wiki/Fuel_efficiency "Fuel efficiency"). In theory, a [combined cycle](/wiki/Combined_cycle "Combined cycle") steam engine in which the burning material is first used to drive a [gas turbine](/wiki/Gas_turbine "Gas turbine") can produce 50% to 60% efficiency. However, practical examples of steam engined cars work at only around 5–8% efficiency.

The best known and best selling steam-powered car was the [Stanley Steamer](/wiki/Stanley_Steamer "Stanley Steamer"). It used a compact fire-tube boiler under the hood to power a simple two-piston engine which was connected directly to the rear axle. Before [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford") introduced monthly payment financing with great success, cars were typically purchased outright. This is why the Stanley was kept simple; to keep the purchase price affordable.

Steam produced in [refrigeration](/wiki/Refrigeration "Refrigeration") also can be use by a [turbine](/wiki/Turbine "Turbine") in other vehicle types to produce electricity, that can be employed in electric motors or stored in a battery.

Steam power can be combined with a standard oil-based engine to create a hybrid. Water is injected into the cylinder after the fuel is burned, when the piston is still superheated, often at temperatures of 1500 degrees or more. The water will instantly be vaporized into steam, taking advantage of the heat that would otherwise be wasted.

### Wind

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Wind")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Quend_16_char_a_voile.jpg/250px-Quend_16_char_a_voile.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quend_16_char_a_voile.jpg)

Wind powered vehicles for recreational purposes

Wind-powered vehicles have been well known for a long time. They can be realized with sails similar to those used on ships, by using an onboard wind turbine, which drives the wheels directly or which generates electricity for an electric motor, or can be pulled by a kite. Wind-powered land vehicles need an enormous clearance in height, especially when sails or kites are used and are unsuitable in urban area. They may be also be difficult to steer. Wind-powered vehicles are only used for recreational activities on beaches or other free areas.

The concept is described in further detail here: [de:Segelwagen](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Segelwagen "de:Segelwagen").

### Wood gas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alternative_fuel_vehicle&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Wood gas")]

Main article: [Wood gas generator](/wiki/Wood_gas_generator "Wood gas generator")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Gasogenog.jpg/250px-Gasogenog.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gasogenog.jpg)

Vehicle with a gasifier

[Wood gas](/wiki/Wood_gas "Wood gas") can be used to power cars with ordinary internal combustion engines if a [wood gasifier](/wiki/Wood_gasifier "Wood gasifier") is attached. This was quite popular during World War II in several European and Asian countries because the war prevented easy and cost-effective access to oil.

As of 2014, Herb Hartman of Woodward, Iowa currently drives a wood powered Cadillac. He claims to have attached the gasifier to the Cadillac for just $700. Hartman claims, "A full hopper will go about fifty miles depending on how you drive it," and he added that splitting the wood was "labor-intensive. That's the big drawback."[[110]](#cite_note-110)
